issues.blurb: Forks and rear shocks need periodic seal and oil service, and skipped intervals lead to leaking seals, lost damping, and stiction.
Dropper seatposts develop sag, slow return, or stuck actuation as internals and cable routing wear and contaminate with dirt.
Disc pads and rotors wear quickly on steep descents and in grit, and worn or glazed pads sharply reduce braking control.
Mud and grit accelerate chain, cassette, and chainring wear far faster than on pavement, so drivetrains need frequent attention.
Suspension pivot and headset bearings take repeated impacts and water, developing play and creaks that need fresh bearings.
Tubeless sealant dries out and tire setups burp or lose air on hard hits, needing regular topping up and pressure checks.

narrative.headline: What mountain bike owners report most
narrative.summary: These are issues commonly reported by mountain bikers as a general reference, not expert-verified data and possibly outdated. Suspension service, the dropper post, and brakes tend to come up most given the rough terrain.

issues.title: Suspension Service (Fork & Shock)
issues.blurb: Commonly reported (~26%) — forks and shocks need seal and oil service; skipped intervals cause leaks and stiction.

issues.title: Dropper Post
issues.blurb: Often reported (~20%) — droppers sag, return slowly, or stick as internals wear and contaminate.

issues.title: Brake Pads & Rotors
issues.blurb: Often reported (~18%) — pads and rotors wear fast on steep, gritty descents.

issues.title: Drivetrain Wear (Mud)
issues.blurb: Reported (~16%) — mud and grit wear chains and cassettes far faster than pavement.

issues.title: Bearing Wear (Pivots & Headset)
issues.blurb: Reported (~14%) — pivot and headset bearings take impacts and water, developing play and creaks.

issues.title: Tubeless
issues.blurb: Reported (~6%) — sealant dries out and setups burp air on hard hits.
